cases.expandedQuery: Actually before we go deeper on that — quick context switch We had a production incident last night where one of our Kubernetes pods got stuck in CrashLoopBackOff and the team spent minutes debugging it before realizing the liveness probe was hitting an endpoint that depended on an external service being up I want to rethink our probe strategy Right now we have liveness and readiness probes on every service pointing at /health which does a full dependency check Should we split those

cases.expandedQuery: Yeah let's do that later Related question though — on the React side we have a dashboard that shows pod health status in real-time via a WebSocket connection The problem is when the WebSocket drops the UI just shows stale data with no indication that it's disconnected I want to add a connection-state indicator and automatic reconnection with exponential backoff We're using TanStack Query for everything else but I'm not sure if it handles WebSocket lifecycle or if I need something separate

cases.expandedQuery: Good approach Switching topics again — we're having a debate on the team about error handling in our data access layer Some people want to use try/catch everywhere and let exceptions bubble up to a global error handler in Fastify Others (including me) want explicit Result types that force callers to handle the error case The argument against Result types is that they're verbose and TypeScript doesn't have pattern matching The argument for them is that you can't accidentally forget to handle an error Where do you land on this for our stack

cases.expandedQuery: No that's good I'll share that with the team Different thing — our GitHub Actions CI pipeline is taking minutes on average and the team is complaining The main bottleneck seems to be the test suite which runs all integration tests sequentially We have about integration tests that each spin up a test database I'm thinking about parallelizing them but I'm worried about port conflicts and shared state We're on GitHub-hosted runners What's the best approach

cases.label: drift
cases.expandedQuery: That's great I'll try the matrix approach Hey one more unrelated thing — we're about to start building the auth service and I need to make a decision on the session token format I know we've been going back and forth on JWT vs opaque session tokens The new constraint is that we need to support token revocation within seconds (compliance requirement) JWTs are stateless which is their whole point so revocation means either short-lived tokens with refresh rotation or a revocation list that every service checks Opaque tokens are naturally revocable since they're just a lookup key But then every API call requires a round-trip to the session store Given our setup which way should we lean

cases.label: drift
cases.expandedQuery: Good analysis I'm leaning opaque too Let me sit on it over the weekend Completely different topic — I've been thinking about our data transformation pipeline We have a bunch of ETL-style jobs that read from MongoDB transform the data and write it back Right now they're written as imperative for-loops with a lot of intermediate variables I want to refactor them to be more functional The transforms are things like filter out inactive users enrich with external data compute aggregates format for the downstream API Each step is independent What's the cleanest way to compose these with our pipe() pattern
